data.title: Cliffs expects Q2 selling prices up about $60 a ton as it targets $425M idled property cash receipts
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "The first quarter of 2026 was the beginning of a sustained improvement progression that will continue through the rest of the year" (Chairman, President & CEO Lourenco Goncalves), while adding that "Q1 results could be better... if not for a couple of one-timers," with "the impact of the spiking on energy costs" the most significant.

  • "Our order book is full and the automotive OEMs are booking more and more steel from Cliffs" (CEO Goncalves), alongside "tight" production schedules and lead times moving out; he said pricing realization has shifted: "Today, that lag is closer to 2 months."

  • "Steel imports into the United States are at their lowest levels, since 2009" (CEO Goncalves), who said, "Section 232 works," and highlighted "recent changes in how derivative products tariffs are being enforced" including "Distribution transformers were added."

  • "In my long career in this business, I have never seen so much momentum in substituting aluminum with steel" (CEO Goncalves), extending beyond autos into "building products, appliances and truck and trailer sectors," and said Cliffs is demonstrating forming steel in equipment "previously utilized exclusive for aluminum."

  • "In February, Cleveland-Cliffs received... Toyota, the Toyota Quality Excellence Award" (CEO Goncalves), saying it confirms quality and execution against Toyota’s standards.

  • "At Burns Harbor, we are idling our smaller plate mill" and "idling the Gary Plate finishing line" (CEO Goncalves), while stating "There will be no loss in overall steel production or layoffs" due to backfilling roles from retiree attrition.

  • "We have also partnered with a leading and prominent AI provider" (CEO Goncalves) to embed AI into production planning and order entry, and added, "We expect to make a full announcement... in the next few weeks."

  • "Our adjusted EBITDA in the quarter was $95 million" (Executive VP & CFO Celso Goncalves), and he said the quarter’s top-line recovery included "shipments totaled just over 4.1 million tons."

Outlook

  • "Q2 shipments are expected to improve from Q1 and remain above that 4.1 million ton mark" (CFO Goncalves), and he framed the trajectory as "Q2 is going to be much better than Q1 and Q3 should be much better than Q2."

  • "Selling prices are expected to be up about $60 a ton from Q1 to Q2" (CFO Goncalves), with the realization lag described as extending "closer to 2 months" as the order book fills.

  • "Our Q2 costs should tick up another $15 per ton higher" (CFO Goncalves), citing higher scrap and fuel and "some scheduled outages in Q2," before costs "falling meaningfully in the back half of the year."

  • "All of our other full year expectations, including volume, CapEx and SG&A remain in line with prior guidance" (CFO Goncalves).

Financial results

  • "First quarter shipments totaled just over 4.1 million tons" (CFO Goncalves), who also reported "Average selling prices increased by $68 per ton from a year ago and sequentially by $55 per ton during the quarter," while noting results were "slightly below our original estimate" due to longer-than-anticipated contractual lags.

  • "The energy spike drove an $80 million negative impact to EBITDA on Q1 relative to historical expectations" (CFO Goncalves), tied to natural gas lock timing plus "electricity and industrial gases."

  • "First quarter free cash flow was negative as expected" (CFO Goncalves), attributing it "primarily due to working capital timing," and said receivables built as "shipments accelerated into March" with pricing higher.

  • "Our $425 million cash receipt expectation from idled property sales remains on target" (CFO Goncalves), adding "2 more properties" went under contract since the prior update and liquidity remained "above $3 billion."

Q&A

  • Carlos de Alba, Morgan Stanley: Asked about Q2 pricing and the remaining impact of the ArcelorMittal Calvert slab tail; Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves replied, "It was 175,000 tons of slabs... but it's over. It's done," and CFO Goncalves added, "Selling prices are expected to be up about $60 a ton from Q1 to Q2" and outlined contract mix including "about 43%" fixed and "15%" Stelco spot.

  • Nick Giles, B. Riley Securities: Asked about working capital unwind and whether POSCO deal terms are firming; CFO Goncalves said the Q1 working capital build was "about $130 million" and Q2 should see "a slight release," while CEO Goncalves said on POSCO, "we are no longer in a hurry" and want "a deal that is accretive for our shareholders."

  • Martin Englert, Seaport Research: Asked about diesel exposure/hedging and aluminum-to-steel switching; CFO Goncalves said, "We don't hedge diesel anymore" and described diesel as "about a $50 million annual impact" ("about $6 per ton"), while CEO Goncalves said substitution is "happening as we speak" and noted, "We brought back the EGL line... at New Carlyle."

  • Nicklaus Cash, Goldman Sachs: Asked about slab math and confidence in Q3 cost drop; CFO Goncalves said, "your math is reasonable" with offsets (scrap and energy) and pointed to Q2 outages versus Q3 being "a very outage light quarter" with utilization-driven "cost dilution."

  • Albert Realini, Jefferies: Asked about trade-offs if POSCO doesn’t materialize versus selling higher-value assets; CEO Goncalves responded, "I don't like playing hypothetical scenarios," adding "shipments continue to go more towards the 16.5 million to 17 million tons for the year" and said HBI supports higher production.

  • Lawson Winder, BofA: Pressed on Q2 cost components and land sale timing; CFO Goncalves cited outages, richer auto mix, and "war-related costs related to diesel and freight," and CEO Goncalves said asset sale contracts are progressing and, on cadence, later guided, "Let's put $50 million in Q2 and $100 million in Q3 with the remainder in Q4."

  • Timna Tanners, Wells Fargo: Asked about plate actions and electrical steel; CEO Goncalves said the idled plate mill was tied to "one specific client" and stated, "the 160-inch mill is now fully utilized," while adding EVs are "the biggest utilization of oriented electrical steels" and said for grain-oriented electrical steels, "there is only one company... that's Cliffs."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical, focusing on bridge items (pricing lag, working capital, cost walk, POSCO optionality), with pointed prompts like "What gives you confidence" (Nicklaus Cash, Goldman Sachs) and requests to "walk us through" scenarios (Albert Realini, Jefferies).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more defensive on specific cost one-timers and hypotheticals in Q&A, including "you should remove for Q1" (CEO Goncalves) on the energy spike and "I don't like playing hypothetical scenarios" (CEO Goncalves) on POSCO alternatives.

  • Versus Q4 2025, management language shifted from "targeting signing a definitive agreement in the first half of 2026" (CEO Goncalves, prior call) to "a deal can be completed within this time frame or slightly a bit later" and "we are no longer in a hurry" (CEO Goncalves, current call).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The current call emphasized near-term operational momentum—"order book is full" and pricing realization lag lengthening—whereas Q4 2025 centered on recovery setup and broader 2026 expectations such as "16.5 million- to 17 million-ton range" shipments and anticipated "unit cost... down another $10 per ton in 2026" (CFO Goncalves, prior call).

  • Canada discussion shifted from Q4’s view that Canadian restrictions "created positive momentum" (CEO Goncalves, prior call) to Q1’s statement that Canada remains "oversupplied" and "the one piece still missing is Canada" (CEO Goncalves), while CFO Goncalves quantified the gap: Canadian selling price at a "40% discount to U.S. pricing."

  • The slab contract narrative moved from Q4’s forward benefit framing ("$500 million" EBITDA order-of-magnitude cited in Q4 Q&A) to Q1’s clean-up conclusion: "175,000 tons" tail shipped and "it's over" (CEO Goncalves).

Risks and concerns

  • "The war activity in Iran has disrupted global freight lanes, driven up energy prices and destabilized metal supply chains" (CEO Goncalves), which management linked to higher energy and freight volatility.

  • "Our Q2 costs should tick up another $15 per ton higher" (CFO Goncalves) due to scrap/fuel pressures and outages, though he said costs should fall in the back half.

  • "One important milestone... is the renegotiation of our labor agreement with United Steelworkers" (CEO Goncalves), describing a need for "competitiveness, flexibility and long-term sustainability" in the agreement.

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as the start of a year-long improvement path, pointing to a full order book, higher automotive pull, extended lead times, and a pricing realization lag that is expected to benefit Q2 and Q3. The quarter included $95 million of adjusted EBITDA and an $80 million EBITDA headwind from a weather-related energy spike, while management guided to Q2 selling price improvement of about $60 per ton, shipments above the Q1 level of just over 4.1 million tons, and a temporary $15 per ton cost step-up tied to outages and input costs before a back-half decline. The call also highlighted operational footprint actions in plate, ongoing POSCO talks with less urgency, a forthcoming AI partner announcement, and a targeted cadence for idled property sale proceeds within the company’s stated $425 million expectation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AnaptysBio rises after completing First Tracks spinoff
data.description:
• Shares of AnaptysBio (ANAB) climbed on Monday after completing the spinoff of its biopharma business, First Tracks Biotherapeutics (TRAXV), which is concurrently making its Nasdaq debut under the ticker symbol “TRAX.”
  • Following the transaction, Anaptys (ANAB) will become a royalty management company focused on its financial collaborations for cancer therapy Jemperli, developed with GSK (GSK), and skin disorder therapy imsidolimab, developed with Vanda (VNDA).
  • “We are now exclusively managing royalties from our out-licensed assets, Jemperli and imsidolimab, with streamlined operations requiring limited FTEs, minimal operating expenses, and providing a greater than 95% EBIT margin,” CEO Daniel Faga remarked.
  • Under the spinoff first announced in September, Anaptys (ANAN) shareholders received one share of First Tracks Bio (TRAX) common stock for each share of Anaptys (ANAB) common stock they held as of April 6.

data.title: WhatsApp testing premium subscription -- TechCrunch
data.description:
Meta Platform’s (META) WhatsApp is testing a subscription service that allows users to pay for cosmetic customizations, including ringtones, themes, and icons, similar to features offered on Instagram Plus and Snapchat+ (SNAP).  

“Premium features include expanded pinned chats, custom lists, new chat themes, and more. We’re starting with a small test to gather feedback and ensure we’re building something people find genuinely valuable,” a Meta spokesperson said to TechCrunch.

Paid messaging on WhatsApp has proven to be lucrative for the parent company, with Meta reporting a 54% increase in revenue from its family of apps in Q4 . Its WhatsApp Business API is expected to generate ~$2B in annual revenue this year, making it one of Meta’s fastest-growing segments.

data.title: Sibanye-Stillwater seeks E.U. concessions as it ramps up Finland lithium mine
data.description:
Sibanye-Stillwater (SBSW) said ‌Monday it is asking for concessions from the European Union to shield Europe's first large-scale lithium mining and processing venture from price volatility and unfair competition, Reuters reported.

Sibanye (SBSW) is advancing its ​Keliber lithium project in Finland in phases as it seeks to manage risk ​in a volatile pricing environment for the metal.

The company began mining ⁠lithium ore at the Syväjärvi open-cast mine in February and plans to commission ​a concentrator during Q3, producing spodumene concentrate at a rate ​of ~140K metric tons/year.

A decision on commissioning a refinery to produce ~15K metric tons/year of battery-grade lithium hydroxide will be taken in Q3 largely ​depends on ongoing talks with the European Union, including on a floor price, Sibanye's (SBSW) chief European advisor Mika Seitovirta told analysts at the company's Capital Day in Finland.

"Could we have some ​price protection mechanism for ​unfair competition, for ⁠instance? That is, of course, something that we need to have," Seitovirta said.

data.title: Wall Street slides as hopes of U.S.-Iran peace deal fade
data.description:
Wall Street traded lower on Monday as optimism over a potential U.S.-Iran peace deal faded, with escalating tensions in the Middle East weighing on investor sentiment and prompting a pullback in equities following last week’s rally.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.3%, while the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.5%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.1%. 

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 1 basis point higher at 4.26%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 2 basis points to 3.73%.

Iran will not participate in a second round of talks with the U.S., according to Iranian state media, which said Tehran objected to what it described as excessive and unrealistic demands from Washington.

U.S. President Donald Trump on Sunday accused Iran of violating a ceasefire agreement after Iranian forces fired upon ships in the Strait of Hormuz, including a French vessel and a British freighter.

In addition, activity through the Strait of Hormuz largely stopped after Iran reimposed control over it, just days before a fragile ceasefire with the U.S. is set to expire, according to Reuters.

Vice President JD Vance, along with the U.S. delegation, is expected to land in Islamabad, Pakistan, on Wednesday, CNN reported.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) are on the rise at $88, while Brent (CO1:COM) also rose to $95 per barrel.

As for stocks that were on the move, Marvell Technology (MRVL) climbed +4.4%, while shares of Intel (INTC) dropped -4.3%.

data.title: Caesars Entertainment gains on report of status of M&A talks
data.description:
Caesars Entertainment (CZR) rose 2.5% after an update on the status of the casino operator's talks with Tilman Fertitta.

Exclusive talks between Caesars (CZR) and Fertitta Entertainment have been extended after a death in the Fertitta family, according to traders, who cited a CTFN report that was circulating on Monday. 

The talks' exclusivity period was extended with the death of Tilman Fertitta's father, Victor Joseph "Vic" Fertitta, Jr., who died on April 9 at the age of 90, according to the report.

The latest update comes after CNBC reported last month that Fertitta was in talks to purchase Caesars (CZR) for $32 a share. The WSJ first reported that Fertitta was in discussions to buy Caesars for $34 a share.

data.title: Meta Platforms set to snap nine-session winning streak
data.description:
Meta Platforms (META) shares are set to snap a nine-session winning streak, as the stock is trading 2.4% lower at $671.75 during afternoon trading hours on Monday.

The company’s shares gained nearly 20% over the past nine sessions. The stock has gained over 13% in the past month.

The tech giant is reportedly planning its first wave of layoffs on May 20, according to sources cited by Reuters last week.

The job cuts will involve roughly 8,000 individuals, representing 10% of the company’s global staff, in what is considered an “initial round” of job cuts, with further reductions expected in the second half of this year.

Looking at Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating, META has a Hold rating with a score of 3.41 out of 5. The company has been graded A+ for profitability and B- for momentum and growth but is weighed down by an F for valuation.

Turning to the Wall Street community, 58 out of 64 analysts rate META a Buy or better, while six analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation. No analysts rate it Sell or worse.

Seeking Alpha analysts are also bullish, rating it a Buy.

Among hyperscalers, Meta Platforms stands out as the company best positioned to prove AI returns, according to two of Seeking Alpha's top tech analysts.

On a recent episode of the Investing Experts podcast, Amrita Roy and Uttam Dey said Meta's AI monetization is considered among the strongest of its peers, with visible revenue acceleration, margin expansion, and forward guidance calling for continued growth.

The tech giant’s shares have gained nearly 2% year-to-date, underperforming the benchmark, which has risen over 4% in the same period.

data.title: QXO’s $17B TopBuild deal provides strategic fit: BNP Paribas
data.description:
QXO’s (QXO) proposed $17 billion acquisition of TopBuild (BLD) drew a favorable response from BNP Paribas, with analyst Seth Weber saying the transaction fits the company’s expansion strategy and supports its goal of building a $50 billion revenue construction-products platform.

Weber maintained an Outperform rating on QXO and a $30 price target following the announcement, describing the deal as broadly consistent with expectations that the company would complete a major acquisition by midyear.

QXO said April 19 that it had reached a definitive agreement to acquire TopBuild (BLD), a leading distributor and installer of insulation and related building products. The transaction would significantly expand QXO’s (QXO) footprint in construction supplies and services, while increasing its presence in commercial and industrial end markets.

For investors, the acquisition represents a major test of QXO’s (QXO) roll-up strategy under Executive Chairman Brad Jacobs, who has said he intends to consolidate the fragmented building-products distribution sector. The deal would sharply increase QXO’s (QXO) scale, but it also raises questions around integration execution, leverage and whether the company can deliver promised synergies in a cyclical construction market.

Under the terms, TopBuild shareholders can elect to receive $505 per share in cash or 20.2 shares of QXO (QXO) stock for each TopBuild share, with cash capped at 45% of total consideration. BNP Paribas said the offer represents a roughly 20% premium to TopBuild’s 60-day volume-weighted average price and a 23% premium to the April 17 close.

Weber said the acquisition would expand QXO’s (QXO) total addressable market to $300 billion from $200 billion and lift annual revenue above $18 billion, including QXO’s (QXO) recently completed Kodiak acquisition and a full-year contribution from TopBuild’s recent deals.

He estimated the purchase price values TopBuild (BLD) at 14.9 times expected 2025 adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization, or 11.8 times after incorporating an estimated $300 million of cost and revenue synergies by 2030.

While the headline valuation sits near the upper end of the range BNP Paribas expects QXO to pay for acquisitions, Weber said that appears justified by TopBuild’s stronger profitability. TopBuild’s projected 2026 adjusted ebitda margin of about 17.8% compares with BNP’s current forecast of 9.1% for QXO.

TopBuild generated $5.4 billion in 2025 revenue, with 55% coming from installation services and the remainder from specialty distribution, according to the report. The company has also outlined a path to $9 billion to $10 billion in annual revenue and $1.7 billion to $2.0 billion in adjusted ebitda by 2030.

BNP Paribas said TopBuild’s operations complement QXO’s existing positions in roofing, waterproofing and lumber-related products, while adding greater exposure to commercial and industrial construction categories where QXO has been less represented.

The transaction has been approved by both boards and is expected to close in the third quarter of 2026, subject to customary conditions. BNP estimates pro forma net leverage in the low-to-mid three-times range after closing.

data.title: Peoples Bancorp dividend preview: 10-year growth streak set to extend in April
data.description:
Peoples Bancorp (PEBO) is expected to announce a dividend increase this April, extending its 10-year streak of consecutive dividend growth.

Based on historical trends, analysts expect a consensus annual dividend of ~$1.67 per share, implying a quarterly dividend of ~$0.4175. This would reflect an increase of ~1.8% from the current ~$0.41 per share distribution.

The company last declared a dividend of ~$0.41 per share in January 2026, translating into an annual yield of 4.85%. Earlier, in April last year, it raised the dividend by ~2.5%, from ~$0.40 to ~$0.41.

This financial holding company has delivered a 5-year dividend growth rate of approximately ~3.51% and currently maintains a payout ratio of ~54.85%.

In terms of dividend quality metrics, the company holds ratings of D+ for dividend safety, C- for growth, B+ for yield, and A- for consistency.

Peoples Bancorp (PEBO) is scheduled to report its Q1 2026 earnings on April 21, 2026, before the market opens.

data.title: Dynex sees spreads potentially trading toward 120 bps as it reports $13.31 estimated book value quarter-to-date
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Dynex Capital, Inc. (DX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We managed the portfolio through a short burst of volatility, which we used to opportunistically raise and deploy capital. We grew the total capital base by 18%, deploying the funds during the quarter as MBS spreads widened. Since quarter end, MBS spreads have tightened and book value is higher." (Co-CEO, President & Director Smriti L. Popenoe)
  • "I'd like to begin by welcoming [ Kaitlyn Mauritz ], who joined Dynex today to lead Capital Markets and Investor Relations." (Chief Financial Officer Michael Sartori)
  • "Book value ended the quarter at $12.60 per share, and economic return was negative 2.5% for the quarter, consisting of $0.51 per share of common dividends and an $0.85 per share decrease in book value." (Chief Financial Officer Sartori)
  • "We ended the quarter with leverage at 8.6x versus total equity" and "our liquidity position remained very strong with $1.3 billion in cash and unencumbered securities at the end of the quarter, representing over 46% of total equity." (Chief Financial Officer Sartori)
  • "We have seen Agency MBS spreads to 7-year interest rate swaps begin to trend tighter again" and "we believe spreads can trade towards 120 again with scope for long-term equilibrium spreads closer to 100 basis points." (Chief Investment Officer Terrence Connelly)

Outlook

  • "As geopolitical events evolve and policymakers refocus on domestic issues like housing, we believe spreads can trade towards 120 again with scope for long-term equilibrium spreads closer to 100 basis points." (Chief Investment Officer Connelly)
  • "Last quarter, I noted that we expected net supply to be $200 billion this year. So far in 2026, it appears supply could come in even lower." (Chief Investment Officer Connelly)
  • "Proposed changes tied to the Basel III endgame could lower the capital cost banks face to hold mortgages" and "financing costs are declining amid the light regulatory regime." (Chief Investment Officer Connelly)
  • The current transcript did not include management EPS or revenue guidance.

Financial Results

  • "Net interest income for the quarter rose from $0.28 per share to $0.40 per share, primarily due to declining financing costs, which fell 33 basis points due to the impact of the Federal Reserve's rate cuts in the fourth quarter." (Chief Financial Officer Sartori)
  • "The majority of the increase was attributable to the growth in our investment portfolio of $6 billion, reflecting the deployment of capital raised during the quarter of $442 million." (Chief Financial Officer Sartori)
  • "With respect to expenses, G&A increased quarter-over-quarter, driven primarily by onetime items" and "we expect overall expenses to normalize in the second quarter with full year expense ratio anticipated to be flat or modestly lower versus year-end as we grow our capital base." (Chief Financial Officer Sartori)

Q&A

  • Bose George, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: "Can we get an update on book value quarter-to-date?" Co-CEO Popenoe: "As of Friday -- Friday's close, the estimated book value was $13.31 per share, net of the accrued common dividend, and that's up 5.6% versus quarter end."
  • Bose George, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: "Is that across the curve or like on a specific point on the curve?" Chief Investment Officer Connelly: "Yes, I'm quoting those spreads both against the 7-year swap point."
  • Trevor Cranston, Citizens JMP Securities, LLC, Research Division: "How high you'd be willing to take leverage" given potential tightening and volatility? Chief Investment Officer Connelly: "Our leverage... did increase to 8.6x..." and "we're going to remain very opportunistic" and "very resolute in our view on those spreads moving from down to as much as 100 basis points."
  • Trevor Cranston, Citizens JMP Securities, LLC, Research Division: asked about "spec pools versus TBAs". Chief Investment Officer Connelly: "We want to avoid" TBAs because they are "very, very refinanceable and callable" and said Dynex is "positioning for owning significantly more pools."
  • Jason Weaver, JonesTrading Institutional Services, LLC, Research Division: asked about "phasing of capital deployment". Co-CEO Popenoe: "when we see that type of move, that's a signal to us to go put accretive capital that we're raising to work" and "making sure that the cost of capital is lower than the return on the capital that we're deploying."
  • Jason Weaver, JonesTrading Institutional Services, LLC, Research Division: asked about valuation discipline. Co-CEO Popenoe: "we're always going to want the shares to trade at a premium to book value" and "I think we actually deserve to trade at a significant premium."
  • Ameeta Lobo Nelson, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: asked about swap spreads and hedge mix. Chief Investment Officer Connelly: "we expected things to be in the 60% to 80% of the portfolio hedged with interest rate swaps" and "we were right around 70% on a DV01 basis at quarter end."
  • Ameeta Lobo Nelson, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: asked whether March widening "test[ed] that backstop thesis" for GSE buying. Chief Investment Officer Connelly: "they have proven to be very value based" and "overall, it is playing out roughly as we expected."
  • Merrill Ross, Compass Point: asked about swap tenors and curve views. Chief Investment Officer Connelly: "we don't predict, we prepare" and said the swap book "shorten[ed] up a little bit in that 3- to 5-year tenor" while emphasizing "our highest conviction is that mortgage yield spread is what we're here to earn."
  • Eric Hagen, BTIG, LLC, Research Division: asked if anything "changed" in willingness to raise capital. Co-CEO Popenoe: "I don't think the principles have changed" and "the raising and deploying don't necessarily have to be simultaneous in nature."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral to slightly positive, focused on near-term book value, leverage capacity, hedge mix, and the durability of the GSE bid, including "Can we get an update on book value quarter-to-date?" (Bose George, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division) and "how high you'd be willing to take leverage" (Trevor Cranston, Citizens JMP Securities, LLC, Research Division).
  • Management tone was slightly positive and opportunity-focused, emphasizing preparedness and regime change, including "we don't predict, we prepare" and "we are on the verge of a significant regime change." (Chief Investment Officer Connelly)
  • Versus last quarter’s emphasis on strong realized returns, this quarter’s tone centered on volatility navigation and positioning, including "we managed the portfolio through a short burst of volatility" (Co-CEO Popenoe) compared with "The total economic return in the fourth quarter was 10.2%" (COO, CFO & Secretary Robert Colligan).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Financial performance shifted from "The total economic return in the fourth quarter was 10.2%" (COO, CFO & Secretary Colligan) to "economic return was negative 2.5%" (Chief Financial Officer Sartori).
  • Balance sheet and liquidity shifted to higher leverage and lower liquidity ratio, from "leverage of 7.3x total equity" and "$1.4 billion in cash and unencumbered securities... over 55% of total equity" (COO, CFO & Secretary Colligan) to "leverage... 8.6x" and "$1.3 billion... over 46% of total equity" (Chief Financial Officer Sartori).
  • Strategy continuity remained centered on policy-driven MBS spread tightening, moving from "it will likely reset the spread regime tighter" (Chief Investment Officer Connelly, prior quarter) to a more specific range: "spreads can trade towards 120... closer to 100 basis points." (Chief Investment Officer Connelly, current quarter)
  • Management roles evolved: last quarter featured "we separated the roles of Chief Financial Officer and Chief Operating Officer" (Co-CEO Popenoe), while this quarter highlighted "[ Kaitlyn Mauritz ]... to lead Capital Markets and Investor Relations." (Chief Financial Officer Sartori)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Global events, most notably the war in Iran, shifted market focus toward geopolitics and drove a sharp increase in volatility." (Chief Investment Officer Connelly)
  • "Agency mortgages traded like much riskier assets" during volatility, which management framed as both a risk and an entry point: "we navigated that volatility constructively and selectively added assets as spreads widened." (Chief Investment Officer Connelly)
  • Prepayment and convexity management remained central, with technology highlighted as a driver: "technology-driven" heterogeneity in prepayment behavior is creating "meaningful dispersion across pools." (Chief Investment Officer Connelly)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a quarter of volatility-driven repositioning: capital was raised and deployed as spreads widened, leverage increased to 8.6x, and book value ended at $12.60 per share, while quarter-to-date estimated book value was cited at $13.31 per share (net of the accrued dividend). Executives repeatedly tied the opportunity set to policy support for housing finance, expectations for tighter MBS spreads over time, and ongoing portfolio shifts away from more callable TBA exposure toward specified pools.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
